comments:
Summary
The paper proposes a routing framework for Wireless Mesh Networks (WMNs) that replaces simple hop distance with a fuzzy-logic-based integrated link cost (throughput, delay, jitter, residual energy) and uses the Big Bang–Big Crunch (BB-BC) optimization to obtain shortest/near-shortest paths. The authors report MATLAB simulation results for networks of various sizes (25–1000 nodes) and discuss scaling and run-time behaviour.

Strengths
- The idea of combining multiple link/node metrics into a single fuzzy-derived cost is sensible and well motivated for WMNs where multiple QoS factors and energy matter.
- BB-BC is a reasonable soft-computing choice among many heuristics; the authors correctly emphasize the need for “good enough” fast solutions in dynamic networks.
- The paper contains a working implementation in MATLAB and presents qualitative observations about scaling.

Major weaknesses and concerns
1) Insufficient methodological detail: The paper omits essential specifications of the fuzzy inference system (membership functions, rule base, fuzzification/defuzzification methods, input/output ranges and normalization). Without these details the integrated cost cannot be reproduced or evaluated.
2) Missing algorithmic detail for BB-BC path encoding: How candidate paths are represented, how new populations are sampled/generated, how infeasible/disconnected paths are handled, and how path fitness is mapped (minimization vs fitness weighting in center-of-mass) are not clearly described.
3) Lack of meaningful baselines and metrics: The evaluation compares BB-BC only implicitly and does not include quantitative comparisons against standard approaches (e.g., Dijkstra on the integrated cost, ETX/ETT-based routing, AODV, or other metaheuristics such as GA/PSO) nor against a simple deterministic shortest-path baseline. Also, the reported results focus on “path cost” and run-time; they do not report network-level performance (packet delivery ratio, end-to-end delay, throughput, energy consumption) that would demonstrate practical benefits.
4) Scalability and practicality concerns: The paper notes that BB-BC can take ~50 minutes for 1000-node networks with 200 iterations — this is impractical for WMNs that require fast adaptation. There is no quantitative discussion of the periodic measurement/aggregation overhead required to compute the integrated cost across the network, nor of communication and computation overheads at nodes.
5) Experimental rigor: No statistical analysis (multiple random seeds, confidence intervals, variance) is shown. Table(s) and figures are referenced but not presented or fully explained in the text excerpt. It is unclear how many runs were performed and whether results are representative.
6) Presentation and repetition: The manuscript contains repeated paragraphs and references, typos and stylistic issues that reduce clarity and professional presentation.

Suggested improvements
- Include full specification of the fuzzy system (membership functions and rule set), and justify parameter choices. Provide either the rule table or representative rules.
- Describe in detail the BB-BC encoding for paths, population initialization, operators for generating new paths, and how infeasible/looping paths are prevented/handled. Clarify the fitness-to-weight mapping used in the center-of-mass calculation.
- Add direct comparisons against deterministic baselines (e.g., Dijkstra using the same integrated cost) and at least one established routing metric/protocol (ETX/ETT/AODV) and one other heuristic (GA/PSO) with runtime and network-level metrics (PDR, delay, throughput, energy consumption). Show tradeoffs between solution quality and computation time.
- Report experimental methodology more rigorously: number of random seeds, means and confidence intervals, and complexity/time scaling plots. Provide the referenced Table 1 and figures with captions and interpretation.
- Address practicality: quantify measurement overhead and propose mechanisms (e.g., clustering or localized optimization) to make the approach usable in large, dynamic WMNs.
- Clean up the manuscript: remove repeated text, fix references, and improve English and structure.

Recommendation
The paper contains an interesting incremental idea (fuzzy-integrated metric + BB-BC) but currently lacks sufficient methodological detail, rigorous evaluation, and baseline comparisons to assess the claimed benefits. I recommend a low acceptability score in this review context and that the work be substantially revised before being considered for a full oral presentation. The paper may be suitable as a poster or a short-contribution after major revisions that address the concerns above.
